skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video. Triggers: remotion, render video from code, tsx to video, react video, programmatic video, remotion render, code to video, animated video, motion graphics code, react animation video
skill.summary: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4.
skill.author: inference-shell

security_audit.summary: Static scanner flagged 40 patterns (32 external_commands, 5 network, 3 crypto). All are FALSE POSITIVES: backticks are markdown code fences in documentation, URLs are legitimate service links (inference.sh, remotion.dev), and no cryptographic code exists. The skill safely wraps the inference.sh CLI for video rendering via external service.

content.user_title: Render videos from React code with Remotion
content.value_statement: Creating programmatic videos traditionally requires complex video editing software or manual animation tools. This skill transforms React/Remotion component code directly into MP4 videos through the inference.sh platform.
actual_capabilities: Render MP4 videos from React/Remotion TSX component code
Support all Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, and interpolate
Configure output resolution, FPS, duration, and codec settings
Generate animated graphics, motion design, and data-driven videos
Create sequence-based animations with timing control
Accept props for dynamic content in video components
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI installation and authentication
Video rendering depends on external inference.sh service availability
Complex animations may have longer rendering times
Limited to Remotion framework capabilities and supported imports

use_cases.title: Marketing teams generating product demo videos
use_cases.description: Create consistent branded video content from template components with dynamic text and data overlays for social media campaigns.

use_cases.title: Developers building animated tutorials
use_cases.description: Transform code examples and UI demonstrations into engaging video tutorials without screen recording or video editing software.

use_cases.title: Data analysts visualizing metrics as video
use_cases.description: Convert dashboards and data visualizations into animated video reports for stakeholder presentations and automated reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Basic fade animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple fade-in text animation with white text on black background that says Hello World over 3 seconds at 1080p.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner users learning basic Remotion animations

prompt_templates.title: Counter animation with progress
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an animated counter that counts from 0 to 100 percent over 5 seconds with a loading indicator, using spring animations for smooth motion.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-sequence title card
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a title card with three text elements that fade in sequentially, each with different timing. Include a colored background and custom typography styling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced users building complex sequence animations

prompt_templates.title: Dynamic data visualization video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a video component that accepts data props and renders animated charts with interpolated values, smooth transitions between states, and custom color schemes based on input parameters.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert users building reusable data-driven video templates

output_examples.input: Simple fade-in text component with 3 second duration
output_examples.output: MP4 video file at specified resolution with smooth opacity transition from 0 to 1 over the first second

output_examples.input: Animated counter with spring physics configuration
output_examples.output: Video showing numeric values incrementing with elastic bounce effect at each number change

output_examples.input: Sequence-based multi-element animation with staggered timing
output_examples.output: Layered video with elements appearing in order, each with independent fade and position animations

best_practices: Use interpolate and spring for smooth, professional animations instead of linear transitions
Keep component code modular by separating animation logic from visual elements
Test animations at target frame rate locally before rendering final video to verify timing
anti_patterns: Avoid inline styles with complex calculations that recalculate every frame
Do not use useState or side effects that conflict with Remotion's rendering model
Never hardcode frame numbers directly; use useVideoConfig for resolution-independent timing

faq.question: What Remotion APIs are supported?
faq.answer: All core Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img components.

faq.question: Do I need to install Remotion locally?
faq.answer: No, the rendering happens on the inference.sh platform. You only need the inference.sh CLI installed and authenticated.

faq.question: What video formats and codecs are available?
faq.answer: Output is MP4 format with configurable codec options. Check inference.sh documentation for current codec support.

faq.question: Can I use React hooks in my components?
faq.answer: Yes, React hooks like useState and useEffect are supported, but should be used carefully within Remotion's rendering constraints.

faq.question: How do I pass dynamic data to my video component?
faq.answer: Use the props parameter in your input to pass any JavaScript object that your component can access as props.

faq.question: What is the maximum video duration?
faq.answer: Duration is configurable via the duration_seconds parameter. Check inference.sh documentation for platform-specific limits.
